learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Two Pointers, Greedy Algorithm, Swapping, Reversal
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine generating all possible arrangements of a password in dictionary order. Given the current arrangement, your task is to generate the very next arrangement without generating every permutation.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine generating all possible arrangements of a password in dictionary order. Given the current arrangement, your task is to generate the very next arrangement without generating every permutation.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Greedy + Two Pointer pattern whenever the problem asks for the next lexicographical arrangement of a sequence.
learning.signals: Whenever the problem asks for the next lexicographically greater arrangement, think of finding the pivot, swapping, and reversing the suffix.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Implement next permutation, which rearranges numbers into the lexicographically next greater permutation. If such an arrangement is not possible, rearrange the numbers into the lowest possible order (ascending). The replacement must be done in-place using only constant extra memory.

examples.explanation: The next lexicographical permutation after [1,2,3] is [1,3,2].
The array is already the largest permutation, so reverse it.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Generate every permutation, sort them lexicographically, locate the current permutation, and return the next one.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Find the first position from the right where the sequence stops decreasing (pivot). Swap it with the next larger element on its right, then reverse the remaining suffix to obtain the smallest possible larger permutation.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: The suffix after the pivot is always in decreasing order.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever the problem asks for the next lexicographically greater arrangement, think of finding the pivot, swapping, and reversing the suffix.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: The suffix after the pivot is always in decreasing order. Reversing it after swapping produces the smallest possible suffix, giving the immediate next permutation in linear time.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever the problem asks for the next lexicographically greater arrangement, think of finding the pivot, swapping, and reversing the suffix.
commonWrongPaths: Finding the pivot from the left.
Sorting the entire array after swapping.
Choosing the wrong element to swap.

commonMisconceptions: Finding the pivot from the left.
Sorting the entire array after swapping.
Choosing the wrong element to swap.

pattern.name: Greedy + Two Pointers
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever the problem asks for the next lexicographically greater arrangement, think of finding the pivot, swapping, and reversing the suffix.

approaches.title: Generate All Permutations
approaches.idea: Generate every permutation, sort them lexicographically, locate the current permutation, and return the next one.

code.java: Backtracking solution omitted because of factorial complexity.

approaches.title: Greedy + Sorting
approaches.idea: Find the pivot and swap it with the next larger element, then sort the suffix.

approaches.title: Greedy + Two Pointers
approaches.idea: Traverse from right to left to find the first decreasing element (pivot). Find the smallest element larger than the pivot from the right, swap them, and reverse the suffix to produce the next lexicographically smallest permutation.
approaches.intuition: The suffix after the pivot is always in decreasing order. Reversing it after swapping produces the smallest possible suffix, giving the immediate next permutation in linear time.
algorithm: Find the first index i from the right where nums[i] < nums[i+1].
If no such index exists, reverse the whole array.
Find the first index j from the right where nums[j] > nums[i].
Reverse the subarray from i+1 to the end.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[1,2,3]
Pivot=2
Swap 2↔3 → [1,3,2]
Reverse suffix → [1,3,2]
Answer=[1,3,2]

visualization.description: Find the pivot, swap, then reverse the suffix.
steps: Find the pivot from the right.

interviewFollowUps: Why is the suffix always decreasing?
Why do we reverse instead of sort?
How would you find the previous permutation?
Can you generate the kth permutation?
interviewTips: Explain why the pivot is searched from the right.
Describe why the suffix is reversed.
State O(n) time and O(1) space.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: The suffix after the pivot is always in decreasing order.

commonMistakes: Finding the pivot from the left.
Sorting the entire array after swapping.
Choosing the wrong element to swap.
